Name: Synthetic opioids
Tagline: Man-made drugs that mimic the effects of natural opioids (such as opium or heroin) but can be much more deadly. Most synthetic opioids, including nitazenes and fentanyl, are class A drugs which means they're illegal to have for yourself, give away or sell. Some synthetic opioids are used in healthcare as pain relievers or anaesthetics, while others have no legitimate uses and are produced and sold illicitly. Synthetic opioids are often mixed with other drugs, especially heroin, which can increase the risk of overdose.
Also_Known_As: Fentanyl, acetylfentanyl, carfentanyl, fentanyls, methadone, nitazenes, oxycodone, tramadol
Quick_How_You_Might_Feel: Euphoric, relaxed and/or confused, sleepy.
Quick_Effects_On_Your_Body: Effects are similar to natural opioids but can be much stronger, especially when mixed with other drugs, with a higher risk of overdose. Side effects can include respiratory depression, nausea and confusion.
Quick_How_Long_It_Takes_To_Work: This depends on how it is taken but the effects can be very quick – quicker than heroin.
Quick_How_Long_The_Effects_Last: How long the effects last and the drug stays in your system depends on how much you’ve taken, your size and what other drugs you may have also taken.
Quick_Common_Risks: Overdose and death. Injecting synthetic opioids can damage your veins and arteries.
Administration: Synthetic opioids can be used in a similar way to natural opioids. They can be injected, sniffed, swallowed or heated and inhaled.
Duration: How long the effects last and the drug stays in your system depends on how much you’ve taken, your size and what other drugs you may have also taken.
Risks: Overdose and death. Injecting synthetic opioids can damage your veins and arteries.

Name: Alcohol
Tagline: Alcoholic drinks, such as beer, wine and spirits
Also_Known_As: Bevvy, Booze
Quick_How_You_Might_Feel: Sociable, chatty, relaxed and/or anxious, aggressive, risk-taking.
Quick_Effects_On_Your_Body: Too much alcohol can mean slurred speech, blurred vision, loss of balance/coordination.
Quick_How_Long_It_Takes_To_Work: Effects kick in within about 10 minutes or so, depending on the strength of your drink and how fast you drink it.
Quick_How_Long_The_Effects_Last: Can stay in your system for several hours - hangover next day.
Quick_Common_Risks: Binge drinking can lead to injuries from falls, accidents or assaults. Long-term effects include damage to the brain, body and organs. Mixing alcohol with benzos or other depressants can increase the risk of death.
Administration: What we mean by alcohol here is alcoholic drinks, such as beer, wine and spirits. The scientific name for the alcohol in these drinks is ethanol or ethyl alcohol. | Spirits usually contain a much higher concentration of alcohol than wine or lager and are normally drunk in smaller measures. | Ready-to-drink ‘mixers’ and ‘alcopops’ may not seem to be strong drinks but they may contain more alcohol than typical bottles or cans of beer or cider. | There is no completely safe level of drinking, but by sticking within these guidelines, you can lower your risk of harming your health. | To keep the risk of harm from alcohol low, men and women are advised not to regularly drink more than 14 units a week. | Don’t ‘save up’ your units to use in one or two days. If you do drink as much as 14 units in a week you should spread this out over three or more days. | If you want to cut down how much you’re drinking, a good way to help achieve this is to have several drink-free days each week. | If you are out for a drink, have something alcohol-free, every other drink.
Duration: How long the effects last and the drug stays in your system depends on how much you’ve taken, your size and what other drugs you may have also taken. | How quickly you feel the effects and how long they last, depend on how much you’ve taken and how quickly, your size, whether you’ve eaten and any other drugs you may have also taken. | Alcohol is broken down by the liver into other compounds at the rate of about 1 unit per hour. Only the liver breaks down alcohol in the body and nothing else, such as drinking coffee or caffeine drinks, will speed that process up, though you may feel more alert. | The short-term effects of alcohol can last for a day or two, depending on how much you drank, including any hangover. | Alcohol and the compounds that alcohol is broken down into by your liver are poisonous and although they are eventually excreted from the system, they have a potentially damaging effect on almost every system of the body, which can result in health damage over time.
Risks: Binge drinking can lead to injuries from falls, accidents or assaults. Long-term effects include damage to the brain, body and organs. Mixing alcohol with benzos or other depressants can increase the risk of death.
Mixing: Mixing drugs is always risky but some mixtures are more dangerous than others.
Law: Class: Legal

Tagline: Synthetic cathinones are a group of man-made substances similar to cathinone, which is a naturally occurring stimulant drug found in the plant, khat . To avoid trouble with the police, sellers marked the cathinones they were selling as ‘not for human consumption’ and sold them as plant food or bath salts. All these substances are now illegal to supply and possess under UK law.

Users should take regular breaks from the dance floor to cool down and watch out for any mates who are on it – as they might not realise they're in danger of overheating or getting dehydrated. In extreme cases, this could even cause death., Drinking too much (including water) can also be dangerous. This is because synthetic cathinones, similar to ecstasy, might stop your body making urine. There is some evidence that suggests that some people have injected 5-IT, although this does not seem to be a common way of taking 5-IT. | Injecting any drug or sharing injecting equipment can be very risky, as it runs the risk of the injector catching or spreading a virus, such as HIV or hepatitis C.
